message.content: Add comprehensive JSDoc comments to every exported function in apps/api/src/service.ts and apps/api/src/agentClient.ts. Document purpose, parameters, return values, thrown errors, and the freshness and fallback branching behavior. Match the doc comment style already used in packages/data/src/sync.ts. Edit the files directly.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)
- oh-my-claudecode:analyst: Pre-planning consultant for requirements analysis (Opus) (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:architect: Strategic Architecture & Debugging Advisor (Opus, READ-ONLY) (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)

- oh-my-claudecode:code-reviewer: Expert code review specialist with severity-rated feedback, logic defect detection, SOLID principle checks, style, performance, and quality strategy (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)

- oh-my-claudecode:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:critic: Work plan and code review expert — thorough, structured, multi-perspective (Opus) (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:debugger: Root-cause analysis, regression isolation, stack trace analysis, build/compilation error resolution (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:designer: UI/UX Designer-Developer for stunning interfaces (Sonnet) (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:document-specialist: External Documentation & Reference Specialist (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:executor: Focused task executor for implementation work (Sonnet) (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:explore: Codebase search specialist for finding files and code patterns (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:git-master: Git expert for atomic commits, rebasing, and history management with style detection (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:planner: Strategic planning consultant with interview workflow (Opus) (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:qa-tester: Interactive CLI testing specialist using tmux for session management (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:scientist: Data analysis and research execution specialist (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:security-reviewer: Security vulnerability detection specialist (OWASP Top 10, secrets, unsafe patterns) (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:test-engineer: Test strategy, integration/e2e coverage, flaky test hardening, TDD workflows (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:tracer: Evidence-driven causal tracing with competing hypotheses, evidence for/against, uncertainty tracking, and next-probe recommendations (Tools: All tools)
- oh-my-claudecode:verifier: Verification strategy, evidence-based completion checks, test adequacy (Tools: All tools except Write, Edit)
- oh-my-claudecode:writer: Technical documentation writer for README, API docs, and comments (Haiku) (Tools: All tools)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- omc-reference: OMC agent catalog, available tools, team pipeline routing, commit protocol, and skills registry. Auto-loads when delegating to agents, using OMC tools, orchestrating teams, making commits, or invoking skills.
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- oh-my-claudecode:ai-slop-cleaner: Clean AI-generated code slop with a regression-safe, deletion-first workflow and optional reviewer-only mode
- oh-my-claudecode:ask: Process-first advisor routing for Claude, Codex, Gemini, Grok, or Cursor via `omc ask`, with artifact capture and no raw CLI assembly
- oh-my-claudecode:autopilot: Full autonomous execution from idea to working code
- oh-my-claudecode:autoresearch: Stateful single-mission improvement loop with strict evaluator contract, markdown decision logs, and max-runtime stop behavior
- oh-my-claudecode:cancel: Cancel any active OMC mode (autopilot, ralph, ultrawork, ultraqa, swarm, ultrapilot, pipeline, team)
- oh-my-claudecode:ccg: Claude-Codex-Gemini tri-model orchestration via /ask codex + /ask gemini, then Claude synthesizes results
- oh-my-claudecode:configure-notifications: Configure notification integrations (Telegram, Discord, Slack) via natural language
- oh-my-claudecode:debug: Diagnose the current OMC session or repo state using logs, traces, state, and focused reproduction
- oh-my-claudecode:deep-dive: 2-stage pipeline: trace (causal investigation) -> deep-interview (requirements crystallization) with 3-point injection
- oh-my-claudecode:deep-interview: Socratic deep interview with mathematical ambiguity gating before explicit execution approval
- oh-my-claudecode:deepinit: Deep codebase initialization with hierarchical AGENTS.md documentation
- oh-my-claudecode:external-context: Invoke parallel document-specialist agents for external web searches and documentation lookup
- oh-my-claudecode:hud: Configure HUD display options (layout, presets, display elements)
- oh-my-claudecode:learner: Extract a learned skill from the current conversation
- oh-my-claudecode:local-build-reminder: Remind the user to rebuild OMC after editing TypeScript when running from a local fork. Triggered automatically by the AI whenever it notices it (or the user) just changed a src/**/*.ts file in an OMC dev install.
- oh-my-claudecode:mcp-setup: Configure popular MCP servers for enhanced agent capabilities
- oh-my-claudecode:omc-doctor: Diagnose and fix oh-my-claudecode installation issues
- oh-my-claudecode:omc-reference: OMC agent catalog, available tools, team pipeline routing, commit protocol, and skills registry. Auto-loads when delegating to agents, using OMC tools, orchestrating teams, making commits, or invoking skills.
- oh-my-claudecode:omc-setup: Install or refresh oh-my-claudecode for plugin, npm, and local-dev setups from the canonical setup flow
- oh-my-claudecode:omc-teams: CLI-team runtime for claude, codex, or gemini workers in tmux panes when you need process-based parallel execution
- oh-my-claudecode:plan: Strategic planning with optional interview workflow
- oh-my-claudecode:project-session-manager: Worktree-first dev environment manager for issues, PRs, and features with optional tmux sessions
- oh-my-claudecode:ralph: Self-referential loop until task completion with configurable verification reviewer
- oh-my-claudecode:ralplan: Consensus planning entrypoint that auto-gates vague ralph/autopilot/team requests before execution
- oh-my-claudecode:release: Generic release assistant — analyzes repo release rules, caches them in .omc/RELEASE_RULE.md, then guides the release
- oh-my-claudecode:remember: Review reusable project knowledge and decide what belongs in project memory, notepad, or durable docs
- oh-my-claudecode:sciomc: Orchestrate parallel scientist agents for comprehensive analysis with AUTO mode
- oh-my-claudecode:self-improve: Autonomous evolutionary code improvement engine with tournament selection
- oh-my-claudecode:setup: Use first for install/update routing — sends setup, doctor, or MCP requests to the correct OMC setup flow
- oh-my-claudecode:skill: Manage local skills - list, add, remove, search, edit, setup wizard
- oh-my-claudecode:skillify: Turn a repeatable workflow from the current session into a reusable OMC skill draft
- oh-my-claudecode:team: N coordinated agents on shared task list using Claude Code native teams
- oh-my-claudecode:trace: Evidence-driven tracing lane that orchestrates competing tracer hypotheses in Claude built-in team mode
- oh-my-claudecode:ultragoal: Durable multi-goal workflow that persists plan/ledger artifacts under .omc/ultragoal and prints Claude /goal handoff text for the active session
- oh-my-claudecode:ultraqa: QA cycling workflow - test, verify, fix, repeat until goal met
- oh-my-claudecode:ultrawork: Parallel execution engine for high-throughput task completion
- oh-my-claudecode:verify: Verify that a change really works before you claim completion
- oh-my-claudecode:visual-verdict: Structured visual QA verdict for screenshot-to-reference comparisons
- oh-my-claudecode:wiki: LLM Wiki — persistent markdown knowledge base that compounds across sessions (Karpathy model)
- oh-my-claudecode:writer-memory: Agentic memory system for writers - track characters, relationships, scenes, and themes
- telegram:access: Manage Telegram channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Telegram channel.
- telegram:configure: Set up the Telegram channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Telegram bot token, asks to configure Telegram, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I have what I need. The reference style in `sync.ts` favors concise, "why"-focused prose. I'll write comprehensive JSDoc that keeps that explanatory voice while adding `@param`/`@returns`/`@throws` tags and documenting the freshness/fallback branching.

Let me add the comments to `service.ts` first.

input.new_string:
/**
 * Validates a recommendation request, optionally refreshes draw data, then generates
 * number combinations and decorates them with agent-authored explanations.
 *
 * Freshness branching:
 * - When `opts.skipLiveSync` is set, no provider call is made: freshness reports
 *   `syncStatus: "skipped"` against whatever draw is currently latest in the store.
 * - Otherwise a live sync runs. On success, the provider's {@link SyncResult} is mapped
 *   through `classifyFreshness`. If the sync throws (e.g. {@link DrawBackfillError} for an
 *   interior gap), we fall back to `syncStatus: "last-good"` and serve the last fully
 *   synced data, tagging `syncErrorKind` with the error's code/name so callers can see why.
 *
 * Fallback branching: numbers always come from the deterministic generator, so `base` is
 * returned with `fallbackUsed: true` if no combinations were produced. When combinations
 * exist, {@link explainWithAgent} supplies explanations and reports its own `fallbackUsed`
 * (true when the agent was unavailable or its response was rejected).
 *
 * @param body - Untrusted request payload; parsed/validated via `recommendationRequestSchema`.
 * @param opts - Optional overrides.
 * @param opts.agentUrl - Base URL of the explanation agent; forwarded to {@link explainWithAgent}.
 * @param opts.skipLiveSync - Skip the live provider sync and serve current store data.
 * @param opts.provider - Provider used for the live sync; defaults to {@link DhlotteryJsonProvider}.
 * @returns The recommendation response: generated combinations, freshness, feasibility,
 *   disclaimers, and per-combination explanations merged in by id.
 * @throws {ZodError} If `body` fails request schema validation.
 * @throws {Error} With `status: 400` if the resolved `targetDrawNo` is not later than the
 *   latest synced draw.
 */
export async function createRecommendations(
  body: unknown,
  opts: { agentUrl?: string; skipLiveSync?: boolean; provider?: LotteryResultProvider } = {},
): Promise<RecommendationResponse> {

 *   `isAscendingSix`, and `results` reports the per-combination verdict.

input.old_string: export async function checkSavedRecommendation(id: string, drawNo?: number) {
input.new_string:
/**
 * Scores a previously saved recommendation against an actual draw result and records the check.
 *
 * Target-draw branching: when `drawNo` is given it is used verbatim. Otherwise the saved
 * recommendation's own `targetDrawNo` is used — we never clamp to an earlier drawn result;
 * if that draw has not happened yet, the call fails rather than persisting a fake check.
 *
 * @param id - Identifier of the saved recommendation to check.
 * @param drawNo - Optional explicit draw number to score against; defaults to the saved
 *   recommendation's target draw.
 * @returns `{ recommendationId, drawNo, ...ranked, checkId }` — the ranking outcome
 *   (matched numbers, bonus match, rank) plus the persisted check row id.
 * @throws {Error} With `status: 404` if the saved recommendation or the target draw result
 *   cannot be found.
 * @throws {Error} With `status: 409` if no `drawNo` is given and the recommendation's target
 *   draw has not been drawn yet.
 */
export async function checkSavedRecommendation(id: string, drawNo?: number) {

input.new_string:
/**
 * Requests per-combination explanations from the agent's `/explain` endpoint, degrading to
 * deterministic local explanations whenever the agent cannot be trusted to answer correctly.
 *
 * Fallback branching — the returned response always covers every requested combination, via
 * one of these paths (each sets `fallbackUsed: true` except the last):
 * - Network/timeout/non-2xx/invalid-schema errors are caught and yield a generic
 *   "Agent unavailable" fallback. The request is bounded by an {@link AbortController} that
 *   aborts after `timeoutMs`.
 * - A successful, schema-valid response whose ids don't exactly match the requested set
 *   (see `hasExactExplanationCoverage`) is rejected in favour of a fallback noting the
 *   id mismatch — we never serve partial or mismatched explanations.
 * - A response that itself reports `fallbackUsed: true` is replaced with local
 *   explanations while preserving the agent's `analysisSummary`.
 * - Only a fully valid, fully covering, non-fallback response is returned as-is.
 *
 * @param request - The combinations (with tags), lucky numbers, and stats to explain.
 * @param opts - Optional overrides.
 * @param opts.agentUrl - Base URL of the agent; defaults to `AGENT_URL` or
 *   `http://localhost:8000`.
 * @param opts.timeoutMs - Abort timeout in milliseconds; defaults to
 *   `AGENT_EXPLAIN_TIMEOUT_MS` or 20000.
 * @returns An {@link ExplainResponse} covering every requested combination. Never throws;
 *   any failure resolves to a deterministic fallback.
 */
export async function explainWithAgent(
  request: ExplainRequest,
  opts: { agentUrl?: string; timeoutMs?: number } = {},
): Promise<ExplainResponse> {

content.text:
Dependencies aren't installed in this environment (`node_modules` missing), so I can't run the compiler. That's fine here — every change is comment-only and adds no code, type references, or imports that TypeScript compiles, so it can't affect typechecking.

## Summary

Added comprehensive JSDoc to all exported functions, matching the concise, "why"-focused prose voice of `packages/data/src/sync.ts` while layering in `@param`/`@returns`/`@throws` tags.

**`apps/api/src/service.ts`** — all 8 exports:
- `createRecommendations` — full doc with dedicated **freshness branching** (skip vs. live-sync success vs. `last-good` fallback on `DrawBackfillError`) and **fallback branching** (deterministic generator vs. agent explanation) sections.
- `saveRecommendations`, `getInternalStatistics`, `getInternalLatestDraws`, `generateInternalCandidates`, `validateInternalCandidates` — purpose, params, returns, and validation throws.
- `runAgentDevPath` — dev proxy, documents the `status: 502` path.
- `checkSavedRecommendation` — documents the **target-draw branching** (explicit `drawNo` vs. saved target, never clamping) plus the 404/409 error cases.

**`apps/api/src/agentClient.ts`**:
- `explainWithAgent` — the four-way **fallback branching** is spelled out (timeout/network/non-2xx/invalid-schema → generic fallback; id-coverage mismatch → fallback; agent self-reported `fallbackUsed` → local with preserved summary; only a fully valid response passes through), noting it never throws.

Used `{@link ...}` cross-references (e.g. `SyncResult`, `DrawBackfillError`, `explainWithAgent`) for navigability — these are doc-only annotations and don't require imports.

content.text:
Done. Added short notes for every thrown error code across both files:

**`apps/api/src/service.ts`** — annotated each `@throws` status with its HTTP meaning and a brief note:
- `400` (Bad Request) — invalid `targetDrawNo`
- `502` (Bad Gateway) — surfaces the upstream agent failure
- `404` (Not Found) — missing saved recommendation or draw result
- `409` (Conflict) — target draw not yet drawn
- (`ZodError` from schema validation left as-is)

**`apps/api/src/agentClient.ts`** — `explainWithAgent` never propagates, so I documented the three internal error signals that each trigger the fallback: `agent <status>` (upstream non-2xx), `agent schema invalid` (failed schema validation), and `AbortError` (timeout).
